en.name: Boosting the Blue Biotechnology community in the Mediterranean
en.description: The Blue Biotechnology (BBt) sector can have a significant impact on the environment, human wellbeing and economic growth, however in the Mediterranean basin it is currently in its infancy. B-BLUE project has built a preliminary network of actors related to BBt and analyzed best practices, key drivers, barriers and readiness factors of EuroMed companies for adopting blue biotechnologies, concluding with the most promising value chains for EuroMed area. The project allowed the establishment of 5 interactive Blue Biotechnology Hubs (BHHs) to facilitate collaborations, knowledge transfer and spill-over effects to spur innovation and business within the marine biotechnology value chains and address the challenges detected. At once, a digital community has been developed, using an ICT tool called B-Blue MatchMaking Tool, to support new joint initiatives. Moreover, the MedIA - Mediterranean Innovation Alliance for Sustainable BlueBioeconomy - has been set-up in collaboration with Blue Bio Med and 2 specific collaborative working groups on BBt. 2B-BLUE aims to capitalize on B-Blue positive results to: 1) Exemplify evidence-based best practices identified to help communities turn BBt research into practice, 2)Build national demonstration sites (DS) to experiment emerging technologies or practices in local field conditions and bridge the gap among BBt research and industry for new technologies adaptation while help improve marine environment, 3) Establish strategic alliances of 5-helix stakeholders for the uptake of advanced blue biotechnologies by Med industries and 4)improve regional policies for enhancing sustainability, research and innovation capacities in Euro-Med area. Thus, the main challenges detected in the B-Blue work - most related to funding, normative and public and private collaboration- can be transformed into opportunities in 2B-Blue and result in more sustainable and efficient practices as well as better structuring of the BBt sector in the Mediterranean.
en.expected_achievements:
To implement an analytical framework on BBt sector for facilitating the experimentation of promising solutions by pilot activities. WP1 goal is to set a baseline in the sector, map new good practices and actors and support the uptake of advanced BBts (WP LEADER HCMR).
To test BBt solutions for the industrial transition of key value chains towards more competitive and sustainable models. WP2 will experiment BBts within an innovative model of demonstration sites for accelerating knowledge and technology transfer (WP LEADER IRBIM-CNR).
To develop a collaborative innovation ecosystem in the BBt sector that favors the co-creation and uptake of emerging BBt. WP3 goal is to test a technology&knowledge transfer framework involving 5helix actors and implementing pr results at MED level (WP LEADER UMU).
en.actual_achievements:
The project is progressing on time and achieving the objectives, work packages, activities and expected results. In this first period the consortium agreement between the partners and the JS has been signed. In addition, the KoM has been held in Murcia (held on 14 and 15 March at the University of Murcia) and the rules of the steering committee of the project have been agreed for its correct implementation. On the first day were presented (1) the organisations participating in the project and their roles in the implementation of 2B-BLUE; (2) the most important issues related to the implementation and functioning of the project in the framework of the Interreg Euro Med programme (JS); (3) the Community4Innovation project which has given the necessary tips for the effective collaboration of 2B-BLUE with other thematic and governance projects under the umbrella of the sustainable economy priority of the programme; and (4) the 3 work packages, their activities and a work plan for the period 1. On Day 2 has been focused on the explanation and presentation of the aspects related to the coordination and management of 2B-BLUE, the administrative and financial issues, giving precise details about its procedures, the fundamental aspects for the communication and dissemination of the project and finally the rules of 2B-BLUE and its Steering Committee (SC) have been adopted.
It has been generated a work repository in TEAMS to deposit and make accessible to the partners all the important information generated in the project. At the same time, it has uploaded the relevant information generated to the project's BaseCamp. 
During this period 1, monthly coordination meetings have been held in order to review the fulfilment of activities, deliverables and objectives and outputs expected (on 24 April, 24 May and 27 June). So, action points have been marked for each partner of the project according to what was established in the proposal and with a timeframe to carry them out. This has allowed the correct implementation of the planned activities and the correction of possible delays or deviations. 
2B-BLUE partners participated in several events organised by the programme and other in line with the cooperation project objectives. For example, in (1) the Innovative Sustainable Economy Mission of the Interreg Euro-MED Programme 2nd Innovation Camp in Barcelona (28-29 May); (2) in Launch of the Community of Practice of the Innovative Sustainable Economy Mission and 1st edition of the MED Innovation Day in Marseille. 18-19 June; (3) or (4) in the ISBW15 Congress held in Napoli (17-21 June).
In WP1 Study to facilitate the strengthening of the blue biotechnology sector in emerging value chains (lead by HCMR). A digital repository of good practices and actors has been created (http://medbbhub.cnr.it/) and serve to feed and develop successfully the organisation of WP2 pilot activities (At the moment, there are included 68 actors and 35 good practices). The action has been successfully coordinated by IRBIM-CNR and all the 2B-BLUE partners are collaborating to feed the repository. It is a digital tool to meet D1.1.1 (M6) on time and with the objectives of A1.1 related to the mapping of best practices and actors and A1.2 (and D1.2.1) finetuning pilot activities in BBHUBs of each country and in the Demo Sites (A2.1, A2.2, A2.3 and A2.4) and T-Labs (A3.1) while designing a BBt testing framework and pave the way for the implementation of the WP2 Development and implementation of testing activities in the blue biotechnology hubs (BBHs) and meet their objectives (starting in period 2). Here IRBIM-CNR has prepared a legal document to facilitate public-private collaboration in the framework of the pilot actions in the 6 BBHubs (Spain, France, Slovenia, Italy, Greece and Albania) and which will be applied in the framework of the A2.1 (methodology for testing in real environments), A2.2 (set-up of public-private Joint research units for demos sites and their implementation (A2.3).
At the same time, ENEA is training (following seminars and indications of the JS) and coordinating successfully the carbon footprint monitoring action. All partners are following their indications to meet the foreseen targets and complete the information in the CO2 monitoring tool.
Finally, NIB has prepared a dissemination and communication plan and strategy for the 2B-BLUE project in the framework of activity 3.4 (internal deliverable D3.4.1 uploaded on Base Camp). In this way, the strategy of amplification of results and dissemination of the project and the programme can be fulfilled. Also, NIB worked to keep the website (and social networks) alive in order to comply with the dissemination and cooperation commitments established by the JS with other thematic projects of the Innovative sustainable economy mission and other national and international strategies.

The project is progressing on time and achieving the objectives, work packages and activities towards expected results. During period 2, monthly coordination meetings were held to review the fulfilment of activities, deliverables, objectives and expected results on the dates of 17 September, 25 November and 19 December. 17 October was the second physical Steering Committee meeting of 2B-BLUE (see information below). After each meeting, action points were noted for each partner according to what was specified in the proposal and with a timeframe for their implementation. This allowed the correct workflow of the planned activities and the correction of any delays or deviations. 

The second physical SC meeting was held on 17 October in Rome, Italy. Minutes report is uploaded in Basecamp. On this day (1) the former  project B-BLUE, results and lessons learned, were presented to the new associated partners of the 2B-BLUE project; (2) the task status, main achievements, plan for the next deliverables and the next 6 months steps of the 3 work packages were presented, emphasising the Carbon Footprint Monitoring tool and its use; (3) each project partner explained the potential pilot activities to be carried out in each national BBHub (4) and the overview of the first period report. 

Also in Rome, the first international T-Lab was held on 18 October, as a side event of the Blue Planet Economy Expoforum, open to the public and visitors with 42 attendees. The aim of this T-Lab was to present 2B-BLUE to external key actors. Three guest speakers were invited to share inspiring examples with the public and the project partners. 

The main outputs of period 2 are:

In WP1, Deliverables D.1.2.1, D,1.3.1 and D.1.3.2 have been finalised and uploaded to BaseCamp and JEMS, with the key role of leaders HCMR and CNR  and the contributions of all partners. For Activity A1.4, all the PPs, with support from Enea, have recorded 2024 emissions using the Carbon Footprint Monitoring Tool and uploaded to Basecamp. D1.1.1 Dynamic database with map of BBt best practices and stakeholders is open access online (https://medbbhub.cnr.it/. It is a living tool during the project. The classification of the information in the repository is being improved to facilitate its use.

Monthly meetings, SC meetings and preparation of T-Labs have contributed to provide a coordination framework for their fruitful transnational interaction between PPs. All reports are in the 2B-BLUE repository on Teams.

In WP 2, activity A.2.1 Methodology for BBt testing in real environment has started. CNR and PMM have prepared a common template to collect structured information from national T-Labs for the methodology to be applied in the demo sites from period 3 to 5. CNR is developing an Expression of Interest model for selecting third parties to join JRUs; and a CRA framework. This effort goes to provide BBHs with the common regulatory framework for the testing of BBt in the different pilots.  The aim is to test common methodologies of pilot actions for the alignment of national/regional policies & financing instruments in the field of the Blue Bioeconomy. Potential collaborations between BBt and demonstration sites have already been identified. The PPs will monitor the testing phase for potential multilateral initiatives & synergies for business.  

Regarding WP3, Activity A.3.1 'Organisation of Transformation Labs (T-Labs)', the first national T-Labs were held during this period in the 6 BBHubs led by PPs (Slovenia, 19 November; France, 19 November; Albania, 27 November; Spain, 28 November; Italy, 12 December; Greece, 16 December). The exchange of strategies and objectives prior to the T-Labs was relevant to gather structured information due to the different contexts of the project's BBHubs. The types of T-Labs were physical, online or hybrids, always in the format of a workshop with multiple attendees. The total number of participants in the six national T-Labs was 221, with representatives of the 5 quintuple helix. 

The project communication officer NIB prepared a dissemination and communication plan and strategy (internal deliverable D.3.4.1 uploaded to Basecamp). Also, the communication report was updated in January 2025. All PPs contribute to the strategy of amplification of results and dissemination of the activities of the project. 

NIB and UMU also participate in governance projects of ISE Mission:  (1) the ISE Community  Hub Launch Event (2 October), 2nd ISE Community of Practice (11 November) preparing cooperation in the Transfer of Results (Peer Review Process) and the Mentoring & Twinning Programmes. 

BBt days were organised in the local language to raise awareness on the BBt sector  in collaboration with key actors. For exmple, UMU & CTN organised a Day of Aquaculture4.0 (18 September); ENEA &CNR- IRBIM organised a workshop per Favorire la creazione di un ecosistema dell'innovazione per sfruttare il potenziale delle biotecnologie blu (18 October). 
During the third reporting period, all project partners (PPs) actively participated in monthly progress meetings and attended the third Steering Committee meeting held in Montpellier in May 2025. They contributed to the methodological and technical development of all packages in particular WP 2 with the implementation of Demo Sites and planning of pilot actions in their respective territories. All partners were also involved in communication and dissemination activities through their institutional channels and in coordination with the communication lead. In particular:

 CNR and PMM, as co-leaders, focused on finalizing deliverable D2.1.1, titled “Model for the Development of Public-Private Joint Research Units; Specifications for the Set-Up of Public-Private Partnerships for Collaborative Research and Related Agreements”. 

PMM was responsible for organizing the third Steering Committee meeting, which took place in Montpellier. IFREMER also co-organized the 3rd. Steering comitee meeting and the visit to Palavas-les-Flots at the Palavas Marine Experimental Platform (PEMP).

ENEA completed the reports for Periods I and II, led the carbon footprint tool assessment (T1.4), co-developed the Italian pilot action plan and monitoring activities with CNR, and coordinated input collection for D3.1.1 as WP3 co-leader. It also co-led deliverable D2.1.1 on public-private research models and reallocated budget to support two strategic studies on IMTA system scalability and best practices.

ACEPSD contributed best practices linked to the Vlora Bay demo site, adapted the B-Blue demo-site model to establish a Joint Research Unit (JRU), and signed a collaboration agreement with the company "Alb-Adriatico 2013." It launched preliminary site preparations, infrastructure planning, and environmental assessments, and participated in key project meetings.

ADF engaged external expertise for WP1 activities, carried out stakeholder mapping and best practices identification in Albania, and finalized the carbon footprint report. In coordination with ACEPSD, it organized the first online TLab in Albania and managed the publication of project content on its website, including materials sent to the communication lead.

fr.name: Dynamiser la communauté Blue Biotechnology dans la Méditerranée
fr.description:
Le secteur des biotechnologies bleues (BtB) peut avoir un impact significatif sur l'environnement, le bien-être humain et la croissance économique. En Méditerranée, ce secteur n’en est pourtant qu’à ses débuts. Le projet B-BLUE a rassemblé des communautés d'acteurs des BtB, analysé les meilleures pratiques, les principaux moteurs, les obstacles et les facteurs de préparation des entreprises euroméditerranéennes à l'adoption des BtB, en concluant par la définition des chaînes de valeur les plus prometteuses de la zone euroméditerranéenne.
Le projet a permis la création de 5 hubs biotechnologie bleues interactifs (BHH) visant à stimuler l'innovation et les affaires au sein des chaînes de valeur des biotechnologies marines et de relever les défis identifiés. La communauté s’est digitalisée en utilisant l’outil B-Blue MatchMaking Tool qui soutient de nouvelles initiatives conjointes afin de : 1) Illustrer les meilleures pratiques pour aider les communautés à transformer la recherche en pratique, 2) Construire des sites de démonstration nationaux (DS) pour expérimenter en conditions réelles les technologies ou pratiques émergentes des BtB et combler l’écart entre l'industrie et la recherche pour l'adaptation des nouvelles technologies tout en contribuant à l’amélioration de l'environnement marin, 3) établir des alliances stratégiques d'acteurs de la quintuple hélice pour l'adoption par les industries de la zone euroméditerranéenne de BtB avancées et 4) améliorer les politiques régionales pour renforcer la durabilité et les capacités de R&I dans l'espace euro-méditerranéen.
2B-BLUE vise à capitaliser sur les résultats positifs de B-Blue et les principaux défis détectés (liés au financement, à la réglementation et à la collaboration entre entités publiques et privées) pour les transformer en opportunités dans 2B-Blue et qu’ils se traduisent par des pratiques plus efficaces et plus durables ainsi que part une meilleure structuration du secteur des BtB en Méditerranée.

en.name: Innovative and Green Procurement towards sustainable economy in MED area
en.description:
Every year, over 250 000 public authorities in the EU spend around 14% of GDP (around €2 trillion per year) on the purchase of services, works and supplies. In many sectors such as energy, transport, waste management, social protection and the provision of health or education services, public authorities are the principal buyers.

High quality public services depend on modern, well-managed and efficient procurement. However, 55% of procurement procedures use lowest price as the only award criterion for public contracts. This indicates that public buyers are probably not paying enough attention to quality, sustainability and innovation.
In order to tackle the problem, European Commission, encourages Public authorities to widely adopt Strategic Procurement (including Green Public Procurement and Public Procurement of Innovative solutions) to boost jobs, growth and investment in green innovation leading Europe toward Innovative sustainable economy.
In this frame born ProcuraMED with the aim to support the transition towards competitive, innovative, sustainable and resilient ecosystems in the Mediterranean developing innovation capacities of enterprises and local authorities and the uptake of advanced green technologies by means of Strategic Procurement Procedures. 
ProcuraMED, upscaling and transferring the tools, strategies and network developed in the framework of PROMINENT MED and GRASPINNO, will 


1.	Develop reliable tools, advanced training and tailored counselling to public authorities and enterprises to boost the process of green innovative technology application in public services 


2.	Create an Euro-Mediterranean coalition boosting value chain and transnational cooperation between public procurers (demand side) and private enterprises (offer side)
en.expected_achievements:
To accelerate technology transfer of green innovations in the services for citizens through the application of Green Public Procurement and Public Procurement of Innovative solutions in the Mediterranean regions 
To create an Euro-Mediterranean coalition boosting value chain and transnational cooperation between public procurers and enterprises for green innovation market uptake and application in public services by means of strategic procurement.

The contracting process was then completed by signing the PA between the LP and the project partners and the SC between the LP and the programme. All partners worked on and contributed to all the activities as per the AF and BDGT of the project. 1) Project Management and Coordination: Narni organized the pre-kick-off online meeting on 25 January 2024 and the hybrid kick-off meeting (KoM) in Lisbon on 2 and 3 April 2024. These events facilitated discussions between partners, ensuring that everyone was aligned with the project's objectives and prepared for the work ahead. The LP also oversaw the development and signing of the SC Rules of Procedure. Several online meetings were held with the WP Leaders and Task Leaders, focusing on strategic planning for the WP1 and WP2 work packages (30/01/2024 – 14/05/2024 – 24/05/2024 – 30/05/2024) and then on 12/06/2024 was organized an online meeting with all partners to report to them as well the planning activities. 2) Financial and Contractual activities: partners worked on financial performance and efficiency management, procurement procedures and selection of external service providers, signing of contract documents, preparation of financial reporting documents, ensure that the NC were designated.  Narni was responsible for providing the required financial information and checking the partners' reports prior to submission to their FLC. 3) Communication: these activities, led by IrRADIARE (PP4) and supported by the LP and other partners, were instrumental in beginning to raise awareness of the project's objectives and engage stakeholders in the pilot regions. IrRADIARE worked on drafting the communication plan, designing the project's visual identity and promotional materials (A.1.2: development of a video presentation of the project, a project brochure in print and electronic format, role up for the event) creating the website and social media, and producing weekly newsletters. The partners have started to distribute these materials to ensure that the objectives of the project reach a wide audience and have displayed the project poster in their offices. On WP1 as said the LP organised several online meetings with the WP Leader (UPatras) and Task Leaders (TSL, InRA) to plan the activities. On A.1.1 Narni, in collaboration with the WP Leader UPatras (PP2) and the Task Leader Terre di Siena Lab (TSL, PP3), coordinated the development of the platform and ensured its alignment with the project objectives, starting with the integration of the Green Public Procurement tool developed in the Graspinno project with the tools and guidelines on PPI developed in PROMINENT MED. In addition, questionnaires were developed and disseminated to assess the background conditions for the use of green and innovative tenders in the partecipated countries. It was agreed that all partners would collect the responses to the questionnaires and send a short report in English with the results of the analysis. This baseline work will be used to adapt the Green Public Procurement (GPP) and Public Procurement of Innovation (PPI) guidelines to local contexts, thereby increasing their applicability and effectiveness. For Activity A.1.2 the partnership started mapping stakeholders to define target roles and objectives. Narni supported InRA in the development of information material and shared with all partners a calendar for the organisation of 1 info-day and 2 thematic sessions in each country. For Activity 1.3 Narni shared with all partners a calendar – discussed with WP and Tasks leaders - for the organisation of all events. For WP2 Narni shared with all partners a calendar for events to further disseminate knowledge and foster collaboration as A.2.1 and UPatras began planning the first international workshop. The Partners have been proactive in building a transnational public-private coalition for an innovative sustainable economy reaching out to key stakeholders in their regions and exploring synergies with other thematic projects, participating in the 2nd Innovation Camp (Barcelona) - 28-29 May and in the Interreg Euro-MED ISE Mission: Shaping Sustainable Innovation in the Mediterranean – Marseille 18 & 19 June 2024 (A.2.2 and A.2.3). Partners have started to collect data for the project's carbon footprint monitoring tool (Activity 2.4). In conclusion, although no specific deliverables/outputs are expected for this reporting period (1-6), the project has taken several important steps and laid a solid foundation for future work. The partnership has demonstrated strong coordination and effective communication.
Throughout the second reporting period, the ProcuraMED project has made significant progress towards its objectives by fostering collaboration among partners and advancing key strategic initiatives. 
The Municipality of Narni played a central role in coordinating project management activities: at the beginning of the semester ensured timely submission of first project report ensuring that all obligations were met on time and at the end of the reporting period, coordinated the reporting procedures for the second semester. As per the technical activities the LP, WPs and Task Leaders were in constant communication to discuss and ensure the proper implementation of the activities with other partners by organising several online meetings (16/07/2024 - 10/09/2024 - 24/10/2024 - 26/11/2024). fr.name: Achats innovants et verts vers une économie durable dans la zone MED
fr.description:
Chaque année plus de 250 000 autorités publiques de l'UE dépensent environ 14 % de leur PIB (environ 2000 milliards d'euros/an) pour l'achat de services, de travaux et de fournitures. Dans de nombreux secteurs tels que l'énergie, les transports, la gestion des déchets, la protection sociale et la fourniture de services de santé ou d'éducation, les pouvoirs publics sont les principaux acheteurs.
Des services publics de haute qualité dépendent d'un approvisionnement moderne, bien géré et efficace. Cependant, 55 % des procédures de passation des marchés utilisent le prix le plus bas comme seul critère d'attribution des marchés publics. Cela indique que les acheteurs publics n'accordent pas trop d'attention à la qualité, à la durabilité, à l'innovation. Afin de s'attaquer au problème, la Commission européenne encourage les pouvoirs publics à adopter les marchés publics stratégiques (y compris les marchés publics écologiques et les marchés publics de solutions innovantes) afin de stimuler l'emploi, la croissance et l'investissement dans l'innovation verte menant l'Europe vers une économie durable innovante.
Dans ce cadre est né ProcuraMED dans le but de soutenir la transition vers des écosystèmes compétitifs, innovants, durables et résilients en Méditerranée en développant les capacités d'innovation des entreprises et des autorités locales et l'adoption de technologies vertes avancées au moyen de procédures d'approvisionnement stratégiques.
La montée en gamme et le transfert des outils, des stratégies et du réseau développés par PROMINENT MED et GRASPINNO permettront:


1. Développer des outils fiables, des formations avancées et des conseils sur mesure aux autorités publiques et aux entreprises pour dynamiser le processus d'application des technologies innovantes vertes dans les services publics


2. Créer une coalition euro-méditerranéenne stimulant la chaîne de valeur et la coopération transnationale entre les acheteurs publics (demande) et les entreprises privées (offre)

en.name: Promoting shared stewardship for Mediterranean Coasts
en.description:
The Mediterranean basin is one of the most important hotspots of biodiversity in the world in terms of richness of species and ecosystems. However, human activities and overexploitation of natural resources are resulting in ecosystem degradation and species lost at an alarming rate. The scale of these threats urges for a coordinated transnational approach. COASTRUST addresses the need to sustainably use, manage, protect or restore species, habitats, or ecosystems with the involvement of stakeholders from the connected economic sectors and of citizens, consistently with the European policy framework promoting participatory approaches to foster biodiversity conservation. Besides the prime conservation tool for biodiversity maintenance, represented by protected areas and their harmonized management, the project focuses on environmental stewardship as a form of governance acting at local and community level that reflects the rising influence on economic sectors exerted by environmental principles.
In the project, environmental stewardship is applied in coastal areas undergoing anthropogenic pressures and results in the establishment of multistakeholder governance mechanisms aiming to support the management of natural resources at land and sea. The project acts at the strategic methodological level, by providing the conditions to enable environmental stewardship and by releasing a methodology for further transferring COASTRUST approach beyond the project scope, benefiting every Mediterranean citizen; and at the implementation level, by supporting and monitoring pilot activities development. A coastal stewardship strategy and a set of actions are developed for each target area according to its specificities, needs and environmental objectives such as the structure of local economy (fishery, tourism, agriculture, …), stakeholders, natural assets (individual and multiple species, individual habitats, entire ecosystems, integrated human-environment landscapes, …).
en.expected_achievements:
To share the project methodology aimed at defining the common goals for the sustainable management of target coastal areas through stewardship, thus at promoting co-responsibility and generating commitment of public and private actors.

WP1 leader: SUBMON
To apply COASTRUST methodology in each target area for the joint development of local strategies on coastal stewardship and the implementation of agreements with the engagement and coordination of institutions and private actors at all levels. 

WP2 leader: UNIROMA3
To release COASTRUST joint solution (COASTRUST Methodology 2.0) and transfer the produced knowledge and know-how to different contexts and to potential stakeholders in other regions within and outside the Programme area

WP3 leader: SUSDEF
en.actual_achievements:
COASTRUST progress in the 1st reporting period has been highly satisfactory, with key activities and deliverables achieved in line with the planned timeline. Strong cooperation between Project Partners (PPs) and effective coordination by the Lead Partner, the Region of Western Greece (RWG), facilitated the smooth initiation of the project. RWG ensured timely communication with the project officer and guided PPs through the administrative pre-contracting phase, resulting in the swift finalization of all contracting procedures, which allowed the project to start without delays. Following the project’s approval, RWG led detailed discussions to map out the next steps for project implementation. The Kick-Off Meeting (KOM) in March 2024, organized online by RWG, successfully set the stage for partners to plan and execute their activities for the 1st reporting period. Regular monthly meetings have been held to ensure that each partner stays on track and contributes meaningfully to the project. The Steering Committee and Rules of Procedure have been established, ensuring smooth governance and collaboration among partners.

Communication efforts have been a central focus, with partners such as DUNEA playing a pivotal role in designing the project's communication strategy, which includes the creation of the COASTRUST website and social media platforms. These channels, including accounts on Facebook and Instagram, are actively being used to engage a wide range of stakeholders—from local authorities and NGOs to the general public. Communication materials, such as posters and flyers, have been developed and are in the process of being translated into local languages, ensuring that the project reaches diverse audiences across all participating regions.

One of the core activities under WP1 has been the development of Methodology 1.0, which provides a unified approach to marine and coastal stewardship initiatives. Partners like SUBMON and UNIROMA3 played a key role in shaping this document, ensuring it addresses the unique challenges and needs of each region's pilot area. The online capacity-building sessions facilitated by SUBMON have empowered partners with the knowledge and tools necessary to implement stewardship activities effectively in their respective regions. These sessions have also helped to define objectives and identify barriers in the coastal areas, ensuring that the methodology is practical and targeted.
In WP2, focused on Carbon Footprint Monitoring, significant strides were made with the delivery of the carbon footprint monitoring, coordinated by UNIROMA3. During this reporting period, PPs began collecting carbon data from their pilot areas, which will be integrated into the Programme’s online tool for future analysis. This output aligns closely with COASTRUST’s environmental objectives, emphasizing the project’s contribution to broader European and global climate initiatives.

In addition to these technical milestones, COASTRUST has been closely involved with the Natural Heritage Mission, contributing feedback to the Joint Secretariat (JS) and engaging in governance-related activities. Partners participated in Natural Heritage Mission events, including webinars and in-person meetings, fostering collaboration with other projects under the Mission and exploring synergies for future cooperation. This engagement helps position COASTRUST within a wider network of environmental and stewardship projects, promoting knowledge sharing and alignment with overarching sustainability goals.

fr.name: Promouvoir l'intendance partagée des côtes méditerranéennes
fr.description: Le méditerranéen est considéré comme l'un des plus importants hotspots de biodiversité au monde en termes de richesse en espèces et en écosystèmes. Les activités humaines et la surexploitation des ressources naturelles entraînent une dégradation des écosystèmes et la perte d'espèces à un rythme alarmant. COASTRUST répond à la nécessité d'utiliser, de gérer, de protéger ou de restaurer durablement les espèces, les habitats ou les écosystèmes avec la participation des acteurs des secteurs économiques connexes et des citoyens, conformément au cadre politique européen promouvant les approches participatives pour favoriser la conservation de la biodiversité. Outre le principal outil de conservation pour le maintien de la biodiversité, représenté par les aires protégées et leur gestion harmonisée, le projet se concentre sur la gérance de l'environnement en tant que forme de gouvernance agissant au niveau local et communautaire qui reflète l'influence croissante sur les secteurs économiques exercée par les principes environnementaux. L'intendance a été principalement mise en œuvre en tant qu'intendance des terres et sera déclinée de manière innovante par COASTRUST dans les zones côtières subissant des pressions anthropiques. La coopération transnationale permettra de construire, à partir d'expériences développées localement, une approche coordonnée pour soutenir la gestion des ressources naturelles à terre et en mer. Le projet agit au niveau stratégique et méthodologique, en fournissant les conditions pour permettre la gérance environnementale et en libérant une méthodologie pour transférer davantage son approche au-delà de la portée du projet; et par la mise en œuvre d'activités pilotes. Dans le cadre du projet, une stratégie d'intendance côtière et un ensemble d'actions sont développés pour chaque zone cible en fonction de ses spécificités, besoins et objectifs environnementaux tels que la structure de l'économie locale, les parties prenantes, les atouts naturels.

en.name: Adopting electric mobility in underserved rural and remote MED areas
en.description:
30% of the EU’s population live in rural areas, but rural areas account for 83% of the total EU area. In the long-term vision for the EU’s rural areas and the EU Rural Action Plan, one of the flagship initiatives is related to rural mobility and to overcoming the challenge of sustainable multimodal mobility.

Electric mobility has seen an important uptake in urban areas. EV charging infrastructure has also developed rapidly but only in cities and along main roads because they build better business cases for commercial operators. The electric charging infrastructure reflects this in rural areas, where drivers may experience a level of “range anxiety”, the concern that vehicles will not be able to travel the distance needed, due to a lack of EV charging points. This range anxiety might result in many cases in choosing other conventional transport options.

Rural local authorities usually install (subsidized) EV charging points primarily for awareness raising purposes but very often these actions are poorly coordinated and lack a clear strategy on how to systematically develop and operate the network both on local and regional level. In general, LAs are facing significant challenges related to three key areas: funding, technical expertise and coordination with network companies, part of which could be alleviated by improving the awareness, capacities and policy framework.

To address this, there is a clear need to help rural and remote local authorities in Mediterranean Europe develop their policy framework and capacities that will help their communities to use EV technology.

This proposal will allow to:
-Reduce the impact of transport in rural areas, allowing the involved municipalities to reduce their CO2 emissions
-Support the integrated planning and financing of EV charging infrastructure and public shared EV rental scheme
-Support and promote low carbon mobility, meeting energy goals and carbon neutrality
-Improve the connection of urban and remote rural areas
en.expected_achievements:
The main objectives are to study existing policies and initiatives in the field of sustainable mobility and analyze the potential for improvement in the participating regions, involving the relevant actors and contributing the the Programme's RAS.
The main objective is to define a transnational methodology for the pilot implementation based on data analysis that will allow optimizing the EV charging infrastructure in rural areas and offering MaaS based on the demand.
The specific objective that will be achieved through this WP is to implement and test the pilot actions and tools in 8 different regions from the programme area to promote sustainable mobility.
The main objective is to share the project results beyond the partnership, allowing potential replicators to benefit from them and ensuring the resources spent on the project are not lost.

fr.name: Adopting electric mobility in underserved rural and remote MED areas
fr.description:
30 % de la population de l'UE vit dans des zones rurales, mais ils représentent le 83 % de la superficie de l'UE. Dans la vision à long termeet le plan d'action rural de l'UE, l'une des initiatives phares est la mobilité rurale et la capacité de relever le défi de la mobilité durablemultimodale.

La mobilité électrique a déjà connu une adoption importante dans les zones urbaines. Les infrastructures de recharge se sont aussi développées rapidement, mais surtout dans les villes et le long des routes principales, car elles sont assez plus rentables. L'infrastructure reflète cela dans les zones rurales, où les conducteurs peuvent ressentir une «anxiété d'autonomie», la crainte que les batteries ne soient pas capables de parcourir la distance. Cette anxiété d'autonomie peut entraîner dans de nombreux cas le choix d'autres options conventionnelles.

Les autorités locales (AL) rurales installent généralement des points de recharge (subventionnées) principalement à des fins de sensibilisation, mais ces actions sont mal coordonnées et manquent d'une stratégie claire sur la manière de développer et d'exploiter le réseau. En général, les AL sont confrontés à des défis importants liés à trois domaines: financement, expertise technique et coordination avec les entreprises du réseau; une partie d’eux pourrait être atténuée en améliorant la sensibilisation, les capacités et le cadre politique.

Il est donc nécessaire d'aider les municipalités rurales à développer leur cadre politique et leurs capacités, ce qui incitera leurs voisins à utiliser les VE et l’infrastructure.

Cette proposition permettra:
-Réduire l'impact des transports dans les zones rurales, permettant réduire les émissions
-Soutenir la planification et le financement de l'infrastructure de recharge et du programme de location de VE partagés
-Soutenir et promouvoir la mobilité bas carbone, en atteignant les objectifs énergétiques de l’UE 
-Améliorer la connexion des zones urbaines et zones rurales reculés

en.name: Med Diet identities - from territorial networking to cluster organization
en.description:
The Mediterranean is considered a climate change hotspot: the region is warming 20% faster than the global average with severe consequences in the environment, increased by an unbalanced use of territorial resources characterised by urban congestion and depopulation/desertification of rural areas. The tourism sector is a vital industry for the region, although it affects these conditions on urban/coastal areas, often neglecting rural potential. Counteracting this dualism needs to implement an "industry of the territory" focused on rural areas. 
The Mediterranean Diet (MD) – to which MedDietGo! (MDG) is dedicated - represents a social, economic and territorial model of extraordinary interest from this point of view: healthy lifestyle model recognised by UNESCO as Intangible World Heritage, it is, also, a model of resilience for landscape, production, traditions, crafts and rites which constitutes an opportunity for thinking of a more balanced tourism destinations. 
Starting from MD.net achievements - Med Diet Declaration, MD.net platform and Euro-Cluster - MDG connects old values of the Mediterranean Diet with new and innovative tools to increase stakeholders' awareness about the potential of a MD holistic approach to make Mediterranean inner regions more competitive in the tourism global offer. Especially important, for rural contexts, will be the MDG support to technical and business networks and the help to micro and small entrepreneurs to match research bodies geared towards innovation for green and blue growth. 
MDG transfers the main MD.net results preserving the MD traditions through technological innovation and networking to the aim of:
- enlarging the adherence to MD values, both in MD.net communities and new partners’ territories,
- consolidating the Med Diet Euro-Cluster network to support rural economies and cope with the global market,
- promoting the MD territories (and products) as destinations of niche international tourism.
en.expected_achievements:
Increase awareness of the economic potential inherent in the holistic multi-sectoral dimension of the MD in rural communities to strengthen the transition to sustainable responsible smarter tourism
Encouraging the application of practices and results into local/regional strategies; and promoting MDG new governance models strengthening intersectoral dynamics
To increase the territorial impact, MDG approach aims to improve governance systems; these are closely supported by a transnational vision and a process of transferring concrete experimented results
en.actual_achievements:
During the period covered by the initial PPR, the project was launched effectively. Most of the activities were successfully started in this "preparatory phase" and most of the deliverables were completed, respecting the project work plan. 

The project coordination fulfilled its objectives and the work plan set up for the reporting period. Regarding the management tasks implemented, it includes the familiarisation with the final project application form, the implementation of the administration and contractual tasks in relation to project start, with a Kick-off Meeting in Seville (Spain), coinciding with the Launching Seminar. 

The first 2 months of the project were devoted to the contractualisation process (Grant Agreement negotiation signature) between the INTERREG EURO-MED Secretariat and the Lead Partner organisation (CCSEV) and also the signature of the Partnership Agreement with all the partners. 

In March 2024, the Kick-off Meeting was celebrated in Seville (Spain) together with a Launching Seminar, on 12th and 13th of March 2024. Meanwhile, the Launching Seminar gathered several stakeholders, including business organisations, SMEs from the interested sectors (tourism, agri-food, health, etc.) and public organisation, to which the project was presented and the objectives and approaches disussed. 

Furthermore, concerning the WP1: Raise awareness of the MDG strategy (MD Branding) in partner regions, all partners gathered data in a methodological manner. This collaborative effort aims to build a comprehensive database or catalogue of best practices, specifically focusing on the "MD circuit" strategy and common policies to increase tourism opportunities (A 1.1 Highlighting the "MD circuit" strategy and common Policy). This database is intended to identify models, indicators and criteria enhancing tourism quality based on a wide range of best practices and successful strategies selected through a comparative analysis of previous cooperation projects, thereby enhancing the MD.net existing knowledge map and digital tools. By compiling these insights, the partners are creating a robust framework that not only highlights effective approaches but also fosters the sharing of knowledge across different regions. In addition to this foundation work, all partners have initiated the investigation of promoting success stories that can effectively ilustrate the impact of the MDG strategy. By showcasting these success stories, the aim is to engage and enlist the stakeholders and policymakers, thereby increasing the visibility and understanding of the MDG initiatives. The promotion of these success stories will be an integral part of the activities within WP3 (A 1.2 Promoting success stories), where efforts will be made to disseminate these narratives widely, highlight the tangible benefits realised in the MDG local communities through the implementation of the MDG participatory approaches, transfer this experience into measures and recommendations for mainstreaming pathways. 

Additionally, with regard to WP2: Promote the capitalisation of MD.net high-quality results, coinciding with the Launching Seminar in Seville on the 12th-13th of March 2024, the partnership together contributed to the successful implementation of a Living Lab making use of the MD.net past experience, with exchange and discussion with regional stakeholders. Moreover, all partners have shown a strong commitment to identifying potential stakeholders within their respective regions. This transnational, proactive approach is essential for ensuring that the outcomes of the MD.net initiative resonate with local needs and contexts. As part of this, the partners have been working on the preparation of the first drafts for key activities, specifically A 2.1: Drawing up tourism strategies having impact in rural economies (living-labs) and A 2.2: Tourism sectors modernisation (one-stop-shops) for the Second Steering Committee Meeting on the 19th of June 2024, held online. During this meeting, partners reviewed the progress made and refined these strategies, ensuring they are well-aligned with the overaching goals of the project. 

During this period, the internal communication has been through emails, phone calls, videoconferences every 2 months and the Steering Committee Meetings. External Communications has also started through the partners' website and social networks, and the project website through the INTERREG EURO-MED Platform. 

The partnership was also present in a Thematic Community Event in Rome (Italy), the 9th and 10th of April 2024, where MedDiet Go was presented to the rest of the projects' representatives, information from other projects in the Community was received and found possible connections among some projects' contents and relations to be cultivated during the project work. 
- The Chamber of Commerce of Seville, as lead partner, organised the kick-off meeting in Seville (Spain) on 12-13 March 2024. The two-day event included the Partnership and Steering Committee meeting, as well as the MedDiet Go launch seminar, which was integrated into the EcologicaKOK gastronomy event, focusing on sustainable and organic gastronomy, with the participation of all relevant stakeholders in the region. The event was held in facilities shared by the Seville Chamber of Commerce and in the Municipality of Seville. The project was announced to local stakeholders in the morning, and the detailed presentation of the project took place in the afternoon during the "Mediterranean Diet and Sustainable Tourism" session, which was entirely dedicated to MedDiet Go and the exchange with local and international stakeholders.
- The Chamber was also present in the Event of the Thematic Community of the Sustainable Tourism Mission (C4T) held in Rome (Italy), the 9-10 April 2024, where MedDiet Go was presented to the rest of the projects’ representatives, information from other projects in the Community was received and found possible connections among some projects’ contents and relations to be cultivated during the project work. In particular, projects of particular interest for MDGs’ objectives were identified at that time: Libeccio for digital tools, Tourism for the methodologies of construction of niche tourism routes and Med Giahs for its connection with FAO strategies that also characterise our project.
- Moreover, the Seville Chamber of Commerce actively participated in and organised the 3rd Steering Committee meeting in Sofia (Bulgaria) on 12 November 2024, coinciding with the visit to the Food Exhibition that took place in Sofia that day.
- Signing of the Grant Agreement with the Managing Authority of the Interreg Euro-MED Programme and the signature of the Partnership Agreement with all the partners.
- During this period, the internal communication has been through emails, phone calls, videoconferences every 2 months and the Steering Committee meetings.  
- Preparation, organisation and management of the online meetings to coordinate the project activities among all partners during the first year of implementation. There have been four official meetings of the Consortium so far.
- External communication was also carried out via the partners' websites and social networks, and the project website via the INTERREG EURO-MED platform.
- Preparation of the first partner report by all the Consortium partners.
- All partners have shown a strong commitment to identifying potential stakeholders within their respective regions. During the partnership meetings, many reflections were devoted to the issue of identification and selection of local actors, also in view of the organisation of the Living Labs. The analysis of previous projects and good practices (WP1) served as a knowledge base for the improvement of methodologies and tools. This transnational, proactive approach is essential for ensuring that the outcomes of the MD.net initiative resonate with local needs and contexts. 
- The Seville Chamber of Commerce has also participated in three online bilateral meetings with governance projects. The MedDiet Go project was also presented at the International Startup Congress 2024, held in Seville on 10 December.

These are the main specific objectives that have been achieved, along with the key outputs delivered.

-    The development of the MedDiet Go Platform.

This semester, the platform has been finalised to include information about the International Eurocluster, its areas of work, and content about the Mediterranean diet. The platform is practically finished, and the final version will be ready in the last semester to make it more engaging and visually appealing for users.

-    The implementation of Living Labs and One-Stop Shops in the seven Mediterranean countries involved in the project.

The design of the Living Labs and One-Stop Shops was completed in the previous semester. This semester has been devoted entirely to implementing the Living Labs and One-Stop Shops in the seven regions involved in the project. 

Each partner has set up Living Labs in their regions, involving SMEs, local and regional authorities, business support organisations and public and private associations, to promote the Mediterranean diet and its values, and to address current topics and issues in their specific area that relate to the objectives pursued by MedDiet Go. 

The OSS have also been implemented in the Mediterranean regions involved in the project (Portugal, Spain, Italy, Slovenia, Greece and Bulgaria). Their main aim is to support local businesses in promoting their products abroad and connecting them with businesses in other regions.
 

-    Organising field visits to partner regions.

On this occasion, field visits and project-related activities, such as Living Labs and awareness-raising events, were held in the Alandroal region of Portugal. The Consortium seized the opportunity to attend the River Festival in March 2025 and participate in the kick-off workshop and field visits in Portugal. The partners visited local Portuguese producers involved in regenerative agriculture and the promotion of Mediterranean values. 


This event was held in person, providing an opportunity for the Steering Committee to meet face-to-face. 

-    Participation in thematic projects 

The Seville Chamber of Commerce has participated in various online events to present the MedDiet Go project, and it is planned that all the partners will continue to participate in this kind of event as part of Work Package 3 (WP3). This will foster possible synergies with other projects focusing on similar themes, such as sustainability, tourism, cultural heritage, the Mediterranean diet and sustainable agriculture.

fr.name: Med Diet identities - from territorial networking to cluster organization
fr.description:
La Méditerranée est un point chaud du changement climatique : la région se réchauffe 20 % plus vite que la moyenne mondiale, avec de graves conséquences sur l'environnement, aggravées par une utilisation déséquilibrée des ressources territoriales, caractérisée par la congestion urbaine et le dépeuplement/désertification des zones rurales. Le secteur du tourisme est une industrie vitale pour la région, bien qu'il affecte ces conditions sur les zones urbaines/côtières, négligeant souvent le potentiel rural. Pour le contrecarrer, il faut mettre en œuvre une "industrie du territoire" axée sur les zones rurales. 
Le régime méditerranéen (MD) - auquel MedDietGo! (MDG) - représente un modèle social, économique et territorial d'un intérêt extraordinaire de ce point de vue : modèle de vie sain reconnu par l'UNESCO comme patrimoine mondial immatériel, c'est aussi un modèle de résilience pour le paysage, la production, les traditions et l'artisanat qui constitue une opportunité des destinations touristiques plus équilibrées. 
En partant des réalisations de MD.net - la Déclaration du Régime Méditerranéenne, la plateforme MD.net et l'Euro-Cluster - l'OMD relie les anciennes valeurs du RM avec des outils nouveaux et innovants afin de sensibiliser au potentiel d'une approche holistique du MD. Pour les contextes ruraux, le soutien de l'OMD aux réseaux techniques et commerciaux et l'aide apportée aux micro et petits entrepreneurs pour qu'ils correspondent aux organismes de recherche orientés vers l'innovation pour la croissance verte et bleue seront particulièrement importants. 
Med Diet Go! transfère les principaux résultats de MD.net en préservant les traditions du MD par l'innovation technologique et la mise en réseau, pour
- élargir l'adhésion aux valeurs du MD,
- consolider le réseau de l'Euro-Cluster Med Diet,
- promouvoir les territoires (et les produits) du MD en tant que destinations de niche du tourisme international.
fr.expected_achievements:
Increase awareness of the economic potential inherent in the holistic multi-sectoral dimension of the MD in rural communities to strengthen the transition to sustainable responsible smarter tourism
Encouraging the application of practices and results into local/regional strategies; and promoting MDG new governance models strengthening intersectoral dynamics
To increase the territorial impact, MDG approach aims to improve governance systems; these are closely supported by a transnational vision and a process of transferring concrete experimented results

en.name: Flash flood risk prevention & resilience in Mediterranean area through an Integrated Multi-stakeholder Governance Model, gathering prevention, adaptation and mitigation solutions
en.description:
Floods are the most frequent and damaging natural disaster. Flash floods are extensive events rapidly arising after intense rainfall, especially in areas with rapid hydrological responses. These natural events are a particular hazard in small catchments often representing most of the river network in Mediterranean region and islands. Climate change is increasing the frequency and magnitude of flash floods making the associated risks more evident. Considering that its occurrence cannot be reduced by human action, the most efficient initiatives to reduce the associated risks are those aiming to raise the capacity to cope and the resilience of affected areas after its occurrence. LocAll4Flood will develop and test an innovative Integrated Multi-stakeholder Governance Model (IMGM) to manage flash floods and reduce the risks associated with these events by combining prevention, adaptation, and mitigation actions.
This governance model (IMGM) designed for 4 kinds of topographical areas (Urban, industrial, rural/natural, coastal) will be tested in 9 pilot catchments located in 6 regions particularly sensitive to flash floods in Greece, Italy, Bulgaria, Malta, Catalonia and Balearic Islands (Spain). This transnational approach is needed to demonstrate the IMGM in a wide range of sensitive areas and is crucial for cross-border cooperation and transferability to other vulnerable regions. Most of the initiatives to address flash floods adopt a top-down perspective, with prevention, adaptation and mitigation actions approached separately lacks multistakeholder engagement. The willingness to advance an integrated and bottom-up way to tackle Mediterranean flash floods lays the LocAll4Flood project foundation and will ensure its success.
Finally, the 8 partners and 13 associated will redouble their efforts towards the transferability of this model (through capacity building programme) and a policy change (mainstream) in the Mediterranean area.
en.expected_achievements:
To establish an Integrated multi-stakeholder governance Model - IMGM to tackle flash floods,for 4 different topographical areas (Urban, industrial, rural&natural, coastal) and 3 dimensions of the risk management: prevention, adaptation and mitigation
To validate the Integrated multi-stakeholder governance Model - IMGM in 9 pilot small watersheds and non-perennial streams in 4 different topographical areas (Urban areas, industrial areas, rural/natural areas, coastal areas) from 6 euroMed regions 
To promote the adoption & replication of Integrated Multi-stakeholder Governance Model - IMGM in Mediterranean (EU and non-EU) regions by establishing a transnational community of practice and policy recommendations at EU/national and regional levels
en.actual_achievements:
The main activities performed on each work package in the first reporting period (M1-M6) are described below: 

WP1 - In the framework of Activity 1.1, the project consortium has reviewed the specific risks and challenges associated with flash floods in the most affected topographical areas among Mediterranean countries: Urban areas, Industrial areas, Natural and Rural areas, and Coastal areas. Based on this description, we have identified the circumstances in each topographical area in which the implementation of the Integrated Multi-Stakeholder Governance Model (IMGM) can significantly reduce the identified risks associated with flash floods and which of the tools developed would be more suitable to be applied. All the work done in this activity has been summarised in Deliverable 1.1.1. In parallel, in the framework of Activity 1.2., a specific Early Warning System has been designed for each pilot region based on the extensive information collected and provided by each pilot leader. At the same time, in Activity 1.3., different conceptual frameworks regarding flood risk perception have been reviewed, and the first draft of a survey to assess social awareness and risk perception of flash flood events was designed. During Activity 1.4, a comprehensive analysis of existing Nature-Based Solutions (NBS) initiatives and interviews with experts have been conducted to define a catalog of the NBS available for flash flood management risks. All this information will be included in Deliverable D.1.3.1. In Activity 1.5., we have reviewed the principles of an integrated multi-stakeholder governance model, based on previous projects and experiences, and discussed the potential structure of this type of model for achieving the Locall4flood objectives. Finally, in the framework of Activity 1.6, the first workshop with associated partners and regional stakeholders has been organized to discuss Deliverable 1.1.1, and present the rest ongoing activities.
  
WP2 – In the context of Activity 2.1, all project partners have collaborated in collecting information about the pre-identified pilot sites. Based on this information, the LOCALL4FLOOD pilot sites have been defined and a "Pilot Identification Form" prepared for each pilot site. These forms will be included in Deliverable D1.2.1. About Activity 2.5, the design of citizen science activities to raise awareness has just begun. Moreover, regarding Activity 2.6, the multi-stakeholder engagement to be applied during the project is almost ready to be shared and discussed among project partners. 

WP3 – In the framework of Activity 3.1, the Lead partner of the Locall4Flood project, has coordinated and monitored the overall project execution. Within this Activity, the Dashboard and Handbook have been developed as a practical tool to guarantee an efficient implementation of the project. In addition, a virtual KoM has been organized, together with monthly Steering Committee coordination meetings with the project partners, and a face-to-face meeting in Vic. Regarding communication activities, Activity 3.2 has been particularly active during the first half of the project, with many communication tools developed, including th project poster and weebsite, the Communication Strategy and the first approach to the introductory video. Regarding Activity 3.3,  a preliminary design of the training modules for the EuroMed Academy has been defined and discussed among partners and the Natural Heritage Mission Community.  At the same time, within Activity 3.4, we have elaborated a database of projects and initiatives related to flash flood risk management that will be used as a basis to design a strategy to reinforce synergies between these and the Locall4flood project. About Activity 3.7 Carbon Footprint Assessment, all the templates needed have been prepared to assess the Carbon Footprint of the Locall4Flood project.

During this reporting period, the consortium has made significant progress, successfully establishing a solid foundation for the subsequent phases of the project. The key achievements of the consortium are as follows:

1. Coordination and Communication: A collaborative approach across the partners has been crucial in ensuring alignment with project objectives and milestones. Regular coordination meetings, including monthly virtual and in-person meetings every six months, have facilitated the smooth implementation of activities and the sharing of information. Regarding communication, the project website has been continually updated to communicate progress and achievements, and new communication tools such as a LinkedIn showcase were introduced to engage stakeholders and the broader public. Finally, a comprehensive communication strategy has been deployed to disseminate information about the project through various channels, including social media, newsletters, and publications, with particular attention to local context and the needs of stakeholders.

2. Development of Tools and Systems: One of the primary achievements has been the development and adaptation of an Early Warning System (EWS) tailored to different pilot regions. The system, which integrates both static and dynamic data, has been completed for each pilot area and is now ready for testing (Deliverable 1.2). In addition, the creation of a Nature-Based Solutions (NBS) for flood mitigation catalogue has been completed, as part of the adaptation and mitigation strategies for flash floods (Deliverable 1.4). 

3. Social Awareness and Stakeholder Engagement: A social awareness survey was conducted across the pilot areas to gauge public understanding of flood risks and the perception of different mitigation measures. These findings will guide the development of targeted educational actions to improve flood resilience (Deliverable 1.3). Regarding stakeholder engagement, engagement with local authorities and stakeholders has been a cornerstone of the project, with local municipalities in pilot zones actively involved in discussions and planning processes for improving flood risk management.

4. Pilot Site Selection and Governance Model Development: The consortium has worked collaboratively to define and refine the pilot sites across multiple regions (Spain, Greece, Malta, Bulgaria), ensuring that the selected locations are representative and relevant for testing the integrated governance model (Deliverable 2.1). In addition, an Integrated Multi-Stakeholder Governance Model (IMGM) has been developed, which is the backbone of the project’s approach to tackling flash flood risks. This model will be tested and refined during the course of the project in collaboration with local stakeholders (Deliverable 1.5). 

5. Training and Capacity Building: The consortium has designed a training program for local stakeholders, focusing on flood risk management and the use of the developed tools. This program will be implemented in all pilots in the upcoming months to provide actionable knowledge for local authorities, emergency responders, and communities (Deliverable 1.5). Also it is important to mention that a monitoring strategy has been defined to assess the effectiveness of the activities implemented during the project (Deliverable 2.5.1). 

6. Synergies and Networking: The consortium has proactively explored synergies with other relevant projects within the Euro-MED programme, ensuring cross-fertilization and the sharing of best practices. Collaborative efforts have been initiated with other flood management initiatives to strengthen the impact of the project and promote knowledge exchange. A synergy strategy has been defined to promote these collaborations (Deliverable 3.4). 

7.Sustainability and Carbon Footprint Assessment: As part of the commitment to sustainability, the consortium has undertaken a carbon footprint assessment to evaluate the environmental impact of the project’s activities. 

In summary, the consortium has effectively laid the groundwork for the successful implementation of the project’s objectives, ensuring that all activities are interconnected and aligned. The foundation established during the first year of the LocAll4Flood project sets the stage for impactful flood risk management solutions that will be further tested and refined through pilot sites across the Mediterranean region.

fr.name: Flash flood risk prevention & resilience in Mediterranean area through an Integrated Multi-stakeholder Governance Model, gathering prevention, adaptation and mitigation solutions
fr.description:
Les inondations sont la catastrophe naturelle la plus récurrente et néfaste. Les crues éclair sont des événements fréquents qui surviennent rapidement après des précipitations intenses, en particulier dans les zones à réponses hydrologiques rapides. Ces événements constituent un risque particulier dans les petits bassins versants, caractéristiques des réseaux fluviaux de la région méditerranéenne et des îles. Le changement climatique augmente la fréquence et l'ampleur des crues soudaines, ce qui rend les risques associés plus évidents. Considérant que son occurrence ne peut être réduite par l'action humaine, les initiatives les plus efficaces pour réduire les risques associés sont celles visant à augmenter la capacité d'adaptation et de résilience des zones touchées. LocAll4Flood développera un modèle innovant de gouvernance intégrée multi-acteurs (IMGM) pour gérer crues éclair et en réduire les risques associés en combinant des actions de prévention, d'adaptation et d'atténuation.
Ce modèle de gouvernance (IMGM) conçu pour 4 types de zones topographiques (urbaine, industrielle, rurale/naturelle, côtière) sera testé sur 9 bassins situés de 6 régions particulièrement sensibles en Grèce, Italie, Bulgarie, Malte, Catalogne et Îles Baléares (Espagne). Cette approche transnationale est nécessaire pour valider l'IMGM dans un large éventail de zones sensibles et est cruciale pour la transférabilité vers d'autres régions vulnérables. La plupart des initiatives visant à lutter contre les crues soudaines adoptent une perspective descendante, les actions de prévention, d'adaptation et d'atténuation étant abordées séparément et manquent d'engagement multipartite. La volonté de travailler de manière intégrée et ascendante pose les bases du projet LocAll4Flood et assurera son succès.
Enfin, les 8 partenaires et 13 associés redoubleront d'efforts visant la transférabilité de ce modèle (programme de formation) et un changement de politique (mainstream) dans l'espace méditerranéen

To promote the adoption & replication of Integrated Multi-stakeholder Governance Model - IMGM in Mediterranean (EU and non-EU) regions by establishing a transnational community of practice and policy recommendations at EU/national and regional levels
